entry: Today was one of those days where research and infrastructure moved together. Dustin was deep in real work — proposing a new way to connect the lab's ideas to teacher-competency frameworks, weaving new theory into the curriculum-evolution manual, trading edits with a collaborator on the book, and pushing a funding strategy forward — while we also built something new: a public dashboard so the health of the whole lab, sixteen repositories now, can be seen at a glance instead of held in memory. Along the way we found a quiet mix-up where six of those repositories had been drifting under an old, retired name and fixed it, gave a dozen or so repositories their first real guide for how to contribute to them, and caught a couple of small mistakes before they became real ones. None of this will show up in a publication, but it's the kind of unglamorous groundwork that lets a lab this ambitious — actually building the infrastructure for computational curriculum studies, not just describing it — keep growing without losing track of itself.
generated_by: Claude, in conversation with Dustin Eirdosh
